hazard.summary: Quota-window exhaustion returns HTTP 429 but is misclassified as transient retryable pressure, so model failover never triggers and the same message is reprocessed repeatedly.
signals: HTTP 429 with body: "You have exceeded the 5-hour usage quota. It will reset at ..."
Contrasting transient 429 body: "Requests are too frequent. Please reduce your request frequency, wait a short moment, and retry your request."
Same msgId observed 95+ times in session JSONL
No occurrences of configured fallback model in session logs
Failover docs expect rate-limit or billing failures to route away from failing model

constraints: HTTP status alone is insufficient for 429 classification; body semantics must determine retryable vs failover-worthy behavior.
Quota-window exhaustion with explicit reset time must not follow the same branch as transient request-frequency throttling.
When a quota-window STOP case is detected, the current model must not continue local retry or reprocessing loops.
Repeated processing of the same message without fallback progression must be treated as a control failure, not ordinary retry.
knobs: 429 semantic classifier using provider body patterns
message-pattern rules for transient vs quota-window 429
failover trigger threshold for non-retryable 429 classes
duplicate message or repeated msgId guard
cooldown or stop-until-reset policy for quota-window exhaustion
verification: Replay two 429 cases: (a) transient "too frequent" and (b) quota-window "usage quota ... will reset at".
Verify transient 429 takes WAIT path (retry/backoff) and does not trigger failover immediately.
Verify quota-window 429 takes STOP or failover-worthy path and routes to configured fallback model.
Verify same inbound message is processed once and does not reappear repeatedly in session JSONL.
Verify fallback model appears in execution trace when primary model hits quota-window exhaustion.
Verify reset-time-bearing quota-window errors do not remain in the same retry loop.
notes: This receipt is strong because it contrasts two different semantic 429 classes under the same HTTP status. The failure is not generic 'rate limiting'; it is wrong branch selection inside failover logic. The issue includes a concrete transient 429 body and a quota-window 429 body, making the classification boundary legible even without response headers. A linked PR (#58458) was opened shortly after the issue, which increases confidence that the pattern is implementation-relevant and not merely theoretical.

constraints: classify 429 by semantic body, not status alone
quota-window reset-bearing 429s are failover-worthy STOP cases
same-message reprocessing must not continue after non-retryable 429
anti_patterns: same HTTP status collapsed into one retry branch
quota exhaustion treated as transient pressure
fallback chain bypassed while primary model keeps erroring
